coredata.dc:title: Heteroatom doped carbon dots from green sources as metal ion sensor and as fluorescent ink
coredata.dc:creator: Mathew, Sneha
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: In this study, novel eco-friendly nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared via a green, efficient, and one-pot synthesis method from Sida cordifolia root as the carbon source and triethylene tetraamine as the nitrogen source for the detection of metal ions. The amorphous, nanosized carbon dots with 17.8 % quantum yield exhibited exceptional stability towards different experimental conditions. These NCDs acted as a nanoprobe for the sensing of Co(II) and Cu(II) via fluorescence and electrochemical techniques, respectively. These blue fluorescent carbon dots detects Co(II) with a detection limit as low as 0.11 μM and Cu(II) with 0.03 nM. The inner filter effect (IFE) acted as the primary fluorescence quenching mechanism. Also, the developed sensor could detect metal ions in different water samples. Moreover, NCDs were used as fluorescent security ink for writing and drawing. The findings of this study open up a new platform for the effective and reliable sensing of metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sida cordifolia
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Co(II)
Cu(II)
Fluorescent ink

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical behaviour of Cu(II) at carbon dot-modified electrode
Analysis of Cu(II) in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Greatly acknowledges the financial assistance received from the University Grants Commission (UGC) in the form of Senior Research Fellowship (SRF).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis., excitation and emission spectra, (b) FT-IR spectrum of NCDs, and (c) wavelength study.
XPS (a) full scan spectrum and high resolution spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s of NCDs.
(a) TEM image, (b) HR-TEM, (c) SAED pattern, and (d) particle size histogram of N-doped carbon dots.
(a) XRD pattern, and (b) Raman spectrum of NCDs.
Stability analysis of NCDs (a) pH, (b) ionic strength, (c) photo irradiation, and (d) time.
(a) Selectivity, (b) concentration, (c) plot of F0/F against concentration, and (d) interference study.
Photographic image of the prepared NCDs solution with and without different metal ion solution under UV light.

Mechanism of sensing of Co(II) (a) absorption spectrum of Co(II) and emission spectra of NCDs, (b), UV spectra of Co(II), NCDs, and NCD + Co(II), and (c) lifetime analysis of NCDs in the presence and absence of Co(II).
(a) CV runs, (b) pH study, (c) scan rate study, and (d) plot of scan rate against current.
(a) Selectivity, (b) DPV, (c) plot of concentration of Cu(II) against current, and (d) interference study (X is all other metal ions other than Cu(II)).
(a) Amperometric current-time response curve, (b) impedance spectrum, (c) stability, and (d) reproducibility studies.
Fluorescent ink applications of NCDs: (a) and (b) images under UV light; (c) and (d) corresponding images under daylight.
Synthesis and applications of N-doped CDs
Comparison of the proposed method with some previous studies for Co(II) detection.
Comparison of the proposed method with some previous studies for Cu(II) detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: DPASV: Differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry; SWASV: Square wave anodic stripping voltammetry.

head.ce:title: Heteroatom doped carbon dots from green sources as metal ion sensor and as fluorescent ink

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, 686560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, novel eco-friendly nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared via a green, efficient, and one-pot synthesis method from Sida cordifolia root as the carbon source and triethylene tetraamine as the nitrogen source for the detection of metal ions. The amorphous, nanosized carbon dots with 17.8 % quantum yield exhibited exceptional stability towards different experimental conditions. These NCDs acted as a nanoprobe for the sensing of Co(II) and Cu(II) via fluorescence and electrochemical techniques, respectively. These blue fluorescent carbon dots detects Co(II) with a detection limit as low as 0.11 μM and Cu(II) with 0.03 nM. The inner filter effect (IFE) acted as the primary fluorescence quenching mechanism. Also, the developed sensor could detect metal ions in different water samples. Moreover, NCDs were used as fluorescent security ink for writing and drawing. The findings of this study open up a new platform for the effective and reliable sensing of metal ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Freshwater system contamination has drawn much attention as a significant environmental danger worldwide and is linked to important environmental issues that humanity is currently facing. Metal ions, such as Cu(II) and Co(II), are nonbiodegradable contaminants that can build up in the environment and in living tissues. Even at very low concentrations, these pollutants can cause a variety of diseases and problems in living things [1]. The trace element cobalt (Co), is an integral part of vitamin B12 and has crucial functions in the human body. In addition to increasing the synthesis of red blood cells, cobalt can also control the catalytic activity of enzymes or cofactors. The deficiency of Co(II) causes retarded growth, anemia, and loss of appetite [2,3]. Cobalt's superior physicochemical qualities make it useful in a variety of fields, including catalysts, mining, paints, batteries, pigments, and more. Cobalt overdose, however, has been linked to a number of illnesses, including asthma, low blood pressure, dermatitis, and even myocardial infarction-related death. But widespread use of cobalt could also result in environmental issues that could be dangerous to human health [4]. The research on Cu(II) also gained significant attraction these years. It is commonly known that Cu(II) is the third most abundant transition element in the living system, following iron and zinc. Cu(II) is essential for the electron transport process in the human body [5]. However, its capacity to produce reactive oxygen species, which take part in cellular metabolism, would impair the biological system if it was in excess [6]. Excessive amounts of copper ions may cause hypertension, liver and kidney damage, neurological disorders, and gastrointestinal problems. Additionally, excessive use in the industry might result in harmful copper discharge and cause environmental and health issues [7]. Due to the adverse effects of heavy metals, they are potential environmental pollutants even at low concentrations. As part of the remediation process, heavy metals should be identified and eliminated from the biological system and aquatic environment [8].

As a result, numerous methodologies have been developed for the analysis of heavy metal ions. Fluorescence (FL) based detection is the most popular among these several sensing technologies because it is affordable, straightforward, and extremely sensitive. Although most techniques are sensitive and selective, they have some downsides, such as extensive sample preparations, specialized people, high costs, and complex costs. However, given selectivity, convenience, sensitivity, real-time detection, and stability towards certain dangerous metals, FL analysis is thought to be more realistically practicable [14]. For the detection of metal ions, a variety of fluorescent probes have been developed, including those based on organic molecules, semiconductor quantum dots, and metal nanoclusters [9–11]. Carbon dots (CDs) are among these fluorescent probes that exhibit considerable potential for the selective detection of heavy metal ions. They are crucially non-toxic, biocompatible, and photostable [12,13].

Natural materials are one of the most widely used precursors for CDs as they are readily available [14–17]. There is still some weakness that has not yet been fully overcome, despite the immense success that has been made. For instance, the reported CDs based on natural materials primarily have poor fluorescence quantum yield (QY). It is generally known that heteroatom doping can significantly alter the characteristics of the resultant CDs making it a convenient technique to tune the physical and chemical properties of the CDs. Recent research has demonstrated that chemical heteroatom doping is a successful method for enhancing the QY, water solubility, fluorescence characteristics, and other physicochemical features of CDs and so broadening the range of applications for these materials [18,19]. It tries to significantly alter the electronic structures of the material, changing their optical and electrical properties and making them suitable for prospective devices. The amount of orbital overlap and the electron-withdrawing/donating capabilities of heteroatoms are thought to have an impact on how the n-states interact in CDs when non-metal atomic impurities and metal ions are introduced [20,21]. Among the different heteroatoms, nitrogen doping is a subject of substantial research. As the N is sufficiently electron rich than C, it provides n-type doping. NCDs (nitrogen-doped CDs) displayed excellent optical characteristics, including strong fluorescence and high quantum yield [22–24]. NCDs have been used in numerous studies as sensing probes for metal ions due to the ease with which metal ions can bind with functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine in CDs, causing a reduction in fluorescence [25,26]. Nitrogen doping considerably increases PL emission of CDs; however, the doping and enhancement mechanism are still unknown [27]. Co-doped CDs such as nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs were reported to exhibit excellent photocatalytic activity [28,29].

In this study, novel Sida cordifolia root-derived carbon dots were first synthesized for the highly selective and sensitive detection of Co(II) and Cu(II). Sida cordifolia, a well-known plant in India also referred to as “Bala” and “Country mallow,” is one of the components used to create many ayurvedic medicines. Sida cordifolia exhibits antibacterial, anticancer, analgesic, antioxidant, antidiabetic, and wound healing properties [30,31]. Also, we extended the application of the synthesized carbon dots as fluorescent ink. Without the usage of any passivating agents or capping, we have used the NCDs for drawing and writing. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of the sensing of Co(II), Cu(II), and as fluorescent ink using nitrogen-doped carbon dots from Sida cordifolia root. Scheme 1
. Shows the shematic representation of the synthesis and applications of NCDs.

The nitrogen-doped carbon dots were synthesized via in situ hydrothermal method. Sida cordifolia root and triethylene tetraamine were mixed and stirred for 5 min. The resultant solution was placed in a 250 mL autoclave and heated at the optimized temperature 200 °C for 10 h. After heating, the mixture was cooled, filtered (0.22 μM), centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min), and stored at 40C. A schematic diagram for the synthesis of NCDs is shown in Scheme 2
.

Sensitivity towards different analytes was evaluated by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of the analytes as a function of wavelength at room temperature. In a typical procedure carbon dots and metal ions having a concentratin of 0.001 M were mixed in a cuvette. It was then sonicated for 4 min, and a fluorescent spectrum was recorded at 380 nm excitation. Similar procdure was adopted for interference studies, where the individual metal ions were added to the NCD and Co(II) mixture.

Electrochemical studies were carried out in PBS solution using a three-electrode system at room temperature. The NCDs in Nafion solution was drop casted on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) to prepare the working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode served as the reference electrode, and the platinum wire acted as the counter electrode.

An empty refill was filled NCDs solution and used for drawing and writing in order to apply as fluorescent ink. The photographs were taken after the images had dried and been illuminated by a UV lamp.

In order to identify the optical properties of the synthesized N-doped carbon dots, the absorption and emission spectra of the NCDs were taken. The absorption spectrum depicted a peak at 280 nm corresponding to the n-π⁎ transition of CO group. On exiting the NCDs at 380 nm, it gives an emission spectrum at 455 nm (Fig. 1a). The surface functional groups of NCDs was analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. The strong, distinctive bands in the range 3330 cm−1 was caused by N-H/O-H stretching vibration. The bands at 1640, 1484, 1323, and 1041 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CO, stretching vibration of the aromatic CN heterocycles, bending vibration of NH group, and bending of CN groups (Fig. 1b) [32,33]. The NCDs were excited from 340 to 400 nm to determine the exact excitation energy. The highest fluorescence intensity was observed at 380 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 1c). Using quinine sulfate as the standard, the quantum yield was measured and found to be 17.8 %, using the relation (1).
(1)
QY S
=
QY R
A R
/
A S
I S
/
I R
ƞ S
/
ƞ R
2
where R and S are the reference and sample, A - absorbance at the excitation wavelength, I - the integrated fluorescence intensity, and ƞ - refractive index of the solvent [34].

Besides, elemental and bond details of NCDs were assessed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). XPS full survey spectrum indicates the presence of C1s, N1s, and O1s in the prepared NCDs with atomic ratios 70.04, 16.16 and 13.8 %. The peaks at 284.9, 398.2, and 531.3 eV in the survey spectrum are assigned for C1s, O1s, and N1s, respectively (Fig. 2a). As the carbon atom present in the NCDs are exposed to three different chemical environments, three peaks are observed in the deconvoluted spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b) at 284.4, 285.9, and 288.4 eV, which describes sp2 hybridized CC, CN, and CO, respectively [35,36]. The deconvolution spectrum of N1s exhibited two peaks at 398.7 and 400.2 eV, which are attributed to the C-N-C and NH groups, respectively (Fig. 2c). The peaks at 530.5, 531.87, and 532.53 eV in the deconvoluted spectrum of O1s correspond to the CO, CO, and C-OH groups, respectively (Fig. 2d) [37]. These results confirm the doping of nitrogen in carbon dots.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) techniques were used to characterize the size and morphology of as-prepared carbon dots. The TEM image reveals that the NCDs are well distributed, and the HR-TEM image shows that the lattice spacing is 0.28 nm (Fig. 3a, b). This matches with the (002) plane of graphene, suggesting that the NCDs contain a carbon core [38]. The broad circular rings exhibited by SAED pattern implies the amorphous nature of the NCDs (Fig. 3c). From the size distribution histogram the average size of NCDs was calculated as 6.30 nm (Fig. 3d). DLS measurements indicated that the NCDs have an average size of 7.78 nm (Fig. S1), which agrees with the TEM results. A single broad peak around 22.850 observed in the XRD pattern of the NCDs corresponds to the (002) inter-layer lattice spacing (Fig. 4a). This is assigned to the disordered carbon atoms and the amorphous nature of the NCDs [39]. In the Raman spectrum of NCDs, distinct D and G bands were observed at 1341 and 1570 cm−1, which may be due to the in-plane vibration of the sp2 hybridized carbon and sp3 defects (Fig. 4b) [32].

The fluorescent stability of the N-doped carbon dots were analyzed in relation to various conditions. The fluorescent intensity of NCDs were checked in different pH solutions (3−10) to examine their stability. Maximum intensity was found at pH 5 (Fig. 5a). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs is remained constant in KCl solution having different concentrations (0–2 M), suggesting that the NCDs can remain stable in a severe salt environment (Fig. 5b). Further, the photostability of the NCDs was investigated to determine the viability of their practical applications. Following 60 min of UV irradiation, NCDs exhibited no noticeable decline in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5c), and steadiness in the fluorescence intensity was observed even after keeping the NCDs at room temperature for 30 days (Fig. 5d). Fluorescence curve for photostability with irradiation time and time is shown in Fig. S2.

Analytical application of the proposed sensor was tested by taking fluorescence spectrum of NCDs in the presence of various metal ions including Cu(II), Cr(VI), Cd(II), Fe(II), Pb(II), Cr(III), Hg(II), Fe(III), Ni(II), Co(II), and Mn(II). Surprisingly, the different metal ions have different effects on fluorescence intensity, but only Co(II) significantly reduced fluorescence intensity compared to the other metal ions, which had little quenching capabilities. Fig. 6a represents relative fluorescence response (F/F0) of various metal ions having the same concentration. Where F and F0 are the fluorescent intensity of NCDs in the presence and absence of various metal ions at 380 nm excitation. Maximum quenching was observed with Co(II), which may be due to the interaction of Co(II) with the functional groups on the surface of NCDs. To obtain the best performance of Co(II) to NCDs, different experimental parameters like incubation time and pH of the detecting system were optimized. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the system remained unchanged after 4 min of incubation with Co(II) (Fig. S3a). For further sensing studies, 4 min of incubation time was chosen. The effect of pH on the sensing system was also optimized. Maximum quenching of fluorescence occurred at pH 6, so further studies were carried out at pH 6 (Fig. S3b). Under these optimized conditions, the sensitivity of the sensor for Co(II) was evaluated by adding an increasing concentration of Co(II) and the fluorescence spectrum was noted. The fluorescence intensity of NCDs gradually decreased with increasing Co(II) concentration (Fig. 6b). The Co(II) concentration showed a good linear response in the concentration range of 0–4.7 μM with a correlation coefficient (Fig. 6c). The limit of detection (LOD) of Co(II) was estimated using the equation LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation and S is the slope of the linear fit as 0.11 μM, which was better than that of the majority of previous reports. There observed a linear relation between Co(II) concentration and fluorescence intensity, i.e., with increase in Co(II) concentration fluorescence intensity showed a linear decrease. Table 1 provides a list of the linear ranges and sensitivity of various fluorescent Co(II) sensors. The findings showed that the as-prepared NCDs in this work were capable of highly sensitive Co(II) detection and had some practical importance.

In addition, the fluorescence intensity seldom ever changes when interfering metals are present in a cobalt mixture. These results demonstrate that the nitrogen doped‑carbon dots are cobalt (II) sensitive even when interfering ions are present (Fig. 6d). This could be due to the faster coordination of Co(II) to the functional groups on the surface of carbon dots and the strong interaction with doped elements like N [40,41]. Because of this, the carbon dots made from Sida cordifolia root is very selective and could be used as cobalt sensors. Photographic image of the prepared NCDs solution with and without different metal ion solution under UV light is shown in Fig. 7 .

Some optical characteristics of NCDs and Co(II) were studied to determine the potential quenching mechanism. The UV–vis. absorption spectrum of Co(II) showed a peak at 500 nm, while the NCD showed an emission wavelength at 450 nm. It was found that the absorption spectrum of Co(II) overlaps with the emission spectrum of the NCDs, (Fig. 8a), indicating that the quenching mechanism might have been the inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The zeta potential of the carbon dot is measured as −22.16 mV (Fig. S4a), the negative zeta potential indicates a negative charge at the surface of NCDs. These negatively charged NCDs interact with positively charged Co(II). In this case, the zeta potential changes to a less negative value (−15.76 mV) (Fig. S4b). The UV–vis. spectra of NCDs, Co(II) and NCDs in the presence of Co(II) were taken to analyze the sensing mechanism in detail (Fig. 8b). Upon the addition of Co(II) to NCDs, no new peaks were appeared or no shift in the peaks were observed. This excludes the possibility of aggregation of NCDs by Co(II) and formation of any ground state charge transfer complex between NCDs and Co(II). Hence, IFE is the most probable mechanism likely responsible for the high sensitivity and selectivity of NCDs towards Co(II) [42]. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of the NCDs were observed in both the presence and absence of Co(II) to determine whether the fluorescence quenching mechanism is the most likely. The lifetime of NCDs in the absence of Co(II) was obtained to be 6.63 ns, whereas in the presence of Co(II) it was reduced to only 6.60 ns (Fig. 8c), a very slight decrease in lifetime indicating FRET was not involved [43]. In light of this, IFE act as the potential quenching mechanism (Table 2 ).

The viability of the proposed sensor was evaluated for the sensing of Co(II) ions in real water samples (lake and river water). The samples were filtered through 0.22 μM Whatman filter paper before analysis. The proposed approach was used to analyze and quantify the Co(II) concentration in the unspiked and spiked lake and river water using the standard addition method. The recoveries were in the range of 99.60–100.22, and the RSD was below 2.5 %. These results demonstrated the high degree of precision and accuracy of suggested method for the detection of Co(II) in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviour of Cu(II) at carbon dot-modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical activity of the developed electrochemical sensor was evaluated by conducting cyclic voltammetric analysis in phosphate buffer solution at bare GCE, NCD/GCE, and Cu(II)/NCD/GCE. In comparison to the bare GCE, the NCD/GCE produced a small current, but on adding Cu(II), the current responses increased drastically (Fig. 9a). This was most likely caused by the effective metal-complexing ability and high electrocatalytic activity of NCDs [49]. The more pronounced anodic peaks for Cu(II) with higher currents were due to the high conductivity and increased electron transfer kinetics across the NCD-modified electrode, which consequently improved the detection signal.

To determine the appropriate pH level, the impact of buffer pH on the electrochemical response of NCDs was examined. Among the different pH (3–10), the peak current reached a maximum at pH 5 (Fig. 9b). Thus, pH 5 is the optimum pH for further studies. The NCDs act as electron donors at this pH, supplying electrons to the copper ions and oxidizing them. The effect of scan rate on the electrochemical potential of NCD/GCE was evaluated at different scan rates (50 to 100 mV/s) with Cu(II) in 0.1 M PBS. It is seen that as the scan rate is increased from 50 to 120 mV/s, the oxidation peak current of Cu(II) increases (Fig. 9c). When the Cu(II) peak current was plotted against the scan rate, a linear graph was obtained (Fig. 9d). Thus the process is a diffused controlled process (Table 3 ).

To successfully verify the effectiveness and application of the NCD-modified electrode, the selective sensing of the heavy metal analytes was carried out using the CV technique. It can be seen that among the different metal ions added (Cd(II), Co(II), Cu(II), Mn(II), Ni(II), Pb(II), Fe(II), Fe(III), Cr(III), Cr(VI) and Hg(II)), only Cu(II) showed significant current responses (Fig. 10a). To investigate how the concentration of Cu(II) ions affect the current signal, DPV measurements were carried out in PBS solutions having varying concentrations of Cu(II). With increasing concentration of Cu(II), a linear increase in the peak current was observed (Fig. 10b). Based on the calibration plot of the concentration of Cu(II) against the current, a linear plot was obtained with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.998. The limit of detection was calculated as 0.03 nM referenced by 3σ/S [50]. This indicates the good sensitivity of the developed NCD sensor (Fig. 10c). Different interfering ions (Cr(VI), Cd(II), Co(II), Fe(II), Hg(II), Mn(II), Ni(II), Pb(II), Fe(III), and Cr(III)) were added to Cu(II) PBS mixture, and signals were noticed. It can be seen from Fig. 10d that the redox potential of Cu(II) @ NCD/GCE was unaffected even in the presence of interfering ions.

Amperometric studies were also carried out to know the conducting power of the Cu(II)/NCD sensor. With increasing concentrations of Cu(II) ions, the current response showed a linear response with time (Fig. 11a). Additionally, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plots of the suggested sensor are shown in Fig. 11b. Because of the high electron-transfer resistance, a significant semicircle of NCDs was adorned on the GCE electrode surface. The long-term stability of the NCD/GCE probe was tested by noting CV measurements of a fresh probe that was kept for one month in a desiccator. The current responses showed not much variations indicating good stability of the NCD/GCE probe (Fig. 11c). Also, the reproducibility tests were done for a cycle of 40, and the current responses have remained almost the same (Fig. 11d). The electrochemical interaction between NCDs and Cu(II) leads to the detection of copper ions by the carbon dots at the optimized pH 5. The oxidation peak is due to the oxidation of Cu(0) to Cu(II), whereas the reduction of Cu(II) to Cu(0) causes the reduction peak [50].

The electrochemical interaction between NCDs and Cu(II) leads to the detection of copper ions by the carbon dots at the optimized pH 5. The oxidation peak is due to the oxidation of Cu(0) to Cu(II), whereas the reduction of Cu(II) to Cu(0) causes the reduction peak [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Cu(II) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: We looked into the recovery tests in different water samples based on the probe's higher sensitivity and strong selectivity towards Cu(II). Fig. 12 depicts the average outcomes. These satisfactory results indicate that the proposed NCD/GCE probe has promising potential for real sample analysis.

Apart from the fluorescent sensing of Co(II), the prepared carbon dots can be used as a brand-new type of security ink. The high water dispersibility, stability, and tunable fluorescent properties of the nitrogen-doped carbon dots stimulated the investigation of fluorescent ink applications [55,56]. We have demonstrated the above properties by using them for drawing patterns and writing letters. The photographs of images of the NCD imprinted on paper is shown in Fig. 13 . These images demonstrate how effectively carbon dot can be used as fluorescent ink. Due to its superior fluorescence qualities, fluorescent ink is currently becoming more and more popular for printing and has the potential to be used in the future for information storage and encryption.

An efficient framework for the detection of metal ions was developed using nitrogen-doped carbon dots. Also, their exceptional fluorescent properties made them useful as fluorescent security ink. The NCDs were synthesized in a cost-effective manner, which exhibited blue fluorescence and excitation dependant fluorescence spectra. In the presence of Co(II), the fluorescence intensity of NCDs quenched significantly, which leads to the quantification of Co(II). The fabricated NCD/GCE-based electrochemical sensor has high potential in sensing Cu(II) in real water samples and detected Cu(II) with a lower detection limit of 0.03 nM. The NCD-based fluorescent security ink finds extensive future application in information storage and encryption. The synthesized NCDs would surely result in significant advancement in sensing and encryption with extremely high resolution and accuracy.
Sneha Mathew: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Validation, Writing – original draft. Beena Mathew: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Greatly acknowledges the financial assistance received from the University Grants Commission (UGC) in the form of Senior Research Fellowship (SRF).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110293.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Monolithic scaffolds for highly selective ion sensing/removal of Co(II), Cu(II), and Cd(II) ions in water
Analyst

New J. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for metal ion detection
